section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: On behalf of Chamber of Progress – a tech industry association supporting public policies to build a more inclusive society in which all people benefit from technological advancements – I write in response to the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) Call for Evidence.

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: We share the Commission's objectives of ensuring a fair, transparent, and competitive digital marketplace, and of protecting consumers, especially minors and vulnerable users, from harmful practices online. These goals are already embedded within the EU's strong legal framework. The challenge today lies not in a lack of regulation, but in the effective, coherent, and consistent application of the laws already on the books.

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: This submission outlines three main considerations for the Commission's ongoing consultation:

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: Europe already has a world-leading consumer protection framework. Existing EU legislation — including the Digital Services Act (DSA), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), Digital Markets Act (DMA), and Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act) — already prohibits the practices the DFA seeks to address. The Commission's focus should therefore be on enforcement and simplification, not duplication.

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: Vague and overlapping concepts risk creating legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict. Undefined terms such as "addictive design," "unfair personalisation," and "consumer vulnerability" would overlap with existing laws, inviting inconsistent enforcement and adding to complexity that reduces Europe's competitive dynamism.

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: Free trials are pro-consumer and pro-competition. They allow users to test digital products and services before committing to purchase, promoting informed choice and market entry for smaller providers. These practices should not be undermined by new restrictions.

section_path: CHAMBER OF PROGRESS
Submission to European Commission Consultation
24 October 2025
paragraphs.text: progresschamber.org | 1390 Chain Bridge Rd. #A108 | McLean, VA 22101 | EU Transparency Register: 882546891537-88

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Europe's consumer-protection architecture is already the most comprehensive in the world. Over the past five years, the Union has enacted an unprecedented suite of legislation covering the full spectrum of digital conduct — from interface design to data processing, advertising, and online marketplaces. Together, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Digital Services Act (DSA), Digital Markets Act (DMA), and Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act) form an interlocking system that prohibits deceptive design, protects against profiling abuses, governs influencer marketing, and secures transparency in digital contracts.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Each of the concerns cited in the Call for Evidence — such as “dark patterns,” “addictive design features,” “unfair personalisation,” “vulnerabilities exploitation,” and difficulties with subscription management — is already regulated by at least one of these instruments. According to a recent study by the law firm Freshfields, “all the key themes potentially addressed by the DFA are already regulated - most being covered by multiple legal acts. In fact, most key themes are regulated by at least 6 legal acts.”² As highlighted in the Joint Industry Statement on the Digital Fairness Act, this body of law “could benefit from clarification and simplification”, not expansion.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: The European Commission's own Fitness Check on EU Consumer Law on Digital Fairness did not demonstrate a systemic gap. Its findings largely identified enforcement and interpretation challenges, not legislative absence. The Regulatory Scrutiny Board similarly cautioned that the underlying evidence base was not robust and pre-dated full implementation of recent legislation. According to the Regulatory Scrutiny Board, the Fitness Check “is not clear on the existence and size of the gap between existing consumer legislation and the digital acquis nor on the scale and development of problems identified. The report does not sufficiently identify and analyse enforcement deficits and does not address the role that Member States national rules and their administrative capacities play.” Furthermore, much of the effects of the overlapping legislation are still unknown because of their recency. As the Freshfields analysis notes “[g]iven the short time they have been in force, their impact and interplay cannot yet be assessed.”

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Proceeding with a new Digital Fairness Act on this foundation risks conflating enforcement gaps with regulatory gaps, an oversight already emphasised in our earlier submission to the Consumer Agenda 2025–2030 Call for Evidence.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Introducing a DFA would therefore duplicate and potentially conflict with existing rules. For example:

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: The DSA’s Article 25 already prohibits deceptive or manipulative interface design; a new DFA clause using different terminology could generate inconsistent compliance expectations.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: The AI Act’s Article 5 bans subliminal or exploitative design practices; re-regulating “addictive features” under the DFA could subject the same design element to two parallel and possibly contradictory tests.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: The UCPD and CRD already address misleading or obstructive subscription models; additional DFA requirements would layer new procedural obligations without clear incremental benefit.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Such overlaps would increase compliance uncertainty and create divergent enforcement across Member States — the opposite of the simplification agenda endorsed by President von der Leyen and supported by the Draghi Report on EU Competitiveness. A dense patchwork of partly overlapping norms not only complicates legal certainty for industry but also diverts public resources away from genuine legislative priorities and policy gaps.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: The Commission’s priority should now be implementation and coherence, not expansion. As our previous submission urged, the Commission should:

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Map and clarify how existing digital and consumer laws interact in practice, using practical joint guidance across the DSA, DMA, GDPR, UCPD, CRD and AI Act.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Strengthen cross-border enforcement through the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network, ensuring consistent outcomes rather than new obligations.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Apply an “evidence-before-expansion” test before any new legislative proposal — requiring a demonstrated market failure that cannot be addressed through interpretation, coordination, or guidance.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
paragraphs.text: Only by consolidating and enforcing the rules already on the books can Europe deliver both consumer fairness and regulatory clarity, while aligning with the Commission’s broader competitiveness and simplification goals.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: The Call for Evidence introduces a series of broad and loosely defined concepts — including “consumer vulnerabilities,” “addictive design features,” “excessive time,” “problematic personalisation,” “unfair personalisation practices,” and “dark patterns.” None of these terms has a settled definition in EU law, and many have already been addressed by existing regulations like the Digital Services Act (DSA), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), and the Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act).

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Creating new categories within the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA) without clear differentiation would not only duplicate these frameworks but risk conflicting legal standards for identical conduct. For example:

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: The DSA (Article 25) already prohibits interface designs that “deceive or manipulate” users or “materially distort or impair” their ability to make free and informed decisions. A parallel DFA definition of “dark patterns” would invite competing interpretations and fragment enforcement across Member States.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: The AI Act (Article 5(1)(b)) bans systems that exploit age-, disability-, or socio-economic vulnerabilities in ways likely to cause significant harm. Introducing a separate DFA notion of “consumer vulnerability” would duplicate this prohibition while forcing digital services to comply with two distinct evidentiary tests for the same behaviour.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Similarly, “addictive design” is already captured under the DSA’s systemic risk-assessment regime and the AI Act’s safeguards against manipulative techniques.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: The Regulatory Fitness Check itself recognised that the EU’s consumer law framework remains largely principle-based, flexible and technologically neutral — attributes that allow regulators to address new practices through

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: interpretation rather than re-legislation. Layering new, undefined terms over this architecture would erode that coherence.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: As the Joint Industry Statement on the Digital Fairness Act cautioned, “further overlapping rules are likely to increase confusion for market participants and authorities alike, reducing the efficiency of enforcement”. This warning is borne out in practice: divergent definitions between instruments could require businesses to maintain separate compliance assessments for each law, increasing cost without improving protection. European companies would face the paradox of more rules, but less clarity — a result contrary to the Commission’s simplification and competitiveness objectives.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Moreover, legal uncertainty carries a direct economic cost. As emphasised in our previous Consumer Agenda 2025–2030 submission, inconsistent terminology across the DSA, DMA, GDPR, and AI Act already creates interpretive complexity for national authorities and companies alike. Introducing yet another layer of definitions would multiply those challenges, fragmenting the Single Market and discouraging firms — particularly start-ups and SMEs — from launching new products in Europe.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: To safeguard coherence and trust, the Commission should:

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Avoid parallel terminology that re-defines concepts already addressed in existing law. Where new guidance is needed, it should be delivered through interpretative communications or CPC-level cooperation, not new legislative clauses.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Maintain a single conceptual taxonomy for manipulative or exploitative practices, drawing directly from the UCPD, DSA and AI Act. Clearly distinguish any conduct regulated by the DFA from conduct already covered by other regulations.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Prioritise guidance and enforcement consistency — ensuring that the same behaviour triggers the same outcome across instruments, jurisdictions, and sectors.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: Practicality, not proliferation, should guide EU consumer policy. Europe’s framework already prohibits harmful design and unfair practices; what is needed now is clarity and convergence, not additional — and potentially conflicting — legislation.

section_path: 1. Europe already has a world leading consumer protection framework
2. Vague and overlapping concepts would create legal uncertainty and regulatory conflict
paragraphs.text: ^{6} https://progresschamber.org/resources/joint-industry-statement-on-the-digital-fairness-act/

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Free trials are a cornerstone of the digital economy. They allow consumers to experience products and services before purchase, helping them assess quality, functionality, and value — particularly for **experiential goods** such as software, media, and digital services. Economically, free trials reduce **information asymmetry**, encourage market entry by smaller providers, and promote informed consumer choice.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: These benefits are already safeguarded and balanced by the EU's existing consumer law framework. The **Consumer Rights Directive (CRD)** requires clear pre-contractual information, explicit consent before payment, and withdrawal rights for distance contracts. The **Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD)** prohibits misleading marketing and aggressive commercial practices, while the **Digital Services Act (DSA)** and **Omnibus Directive** reinforce transparency and ease of cancellation. Together, these rules ensure that any conversion from a free trial to a paid subscription must be both transparent and revocable — protecting consumers without removing the trial option itself.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Introducing new or stricter restrictions under a **Digital Fairness Act (DFA)** would therefore risk **eliminating a pro-consumer practice**. If the conversion from free to paid were over-regulated — for example, by requiring redundant re-consent steps or banning auto-renewals even when clearly disclosed — many digital services would simply withdraw free trials altogether. This would harm consumers, reduce competitive pressure, and entrench incumbents with brand recognition or larger marketing budgets. Free trials are both a legitimate marketing tool and a mechanism that enhances consumer empowerment by allowing experimentation without risk or commitment, and should not be treated with suspicion.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Moreover, enforcement precedent already demonstrates that existing law works. National authorities and the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) network have successfully acted against non-compliant practices — such as unclear auto-renewal terms or obstructed cancellations — under the CRD and UCPD. These actions confirm that **better enforcement**, not new law, is the correct route to protect consumers.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: The economic and policy logic is straightforward: when consumers can test before they pay, they buy with confidence; when firms can offer that flexibility, markets stay competitive. The DFA should not disturb this equilibrium.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: To preserve value for consumers and maintain competitive neutrality, the Commission should:

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Recognise free trials as a legitimate and protected form of marketing that promotes informed choice and lowers barriers to entry for new services.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Rely on existing law — particularly the CRD, UCPD, and DSA — to ensure transparency and fairness in subscription practices.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: Clarify through guidance, not new rules, that compliant free-trial models (with clear disclosure, timely reminders, and straightforward cancellation) fully meet EU consumer-protection standards.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
paragraphs.text: By reinforcing confidence in these established rules rather than layering new obligations, the EU can protect consumers, reward responsible innovation, and support Europe's digital competitiveness.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: The objectives of the Digital Fairness Act — stronger consumer protection, greater trust online, and a more competitive Single Market — are ones we fully share. The question is not whether Europe should protect its consumers, but how it can do so most effectively, without adding unnecessary complexity or undermining innovation.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: The EU already possesses the tools to deliver fairness and accountability in the digital environment. Europe already has the strongest consumer protection framework in the world, with recent regulations still coming into force. What is needed now is coordination, clarity, and consistent enforcement of that framework — not a new additional layer of rules that could stifle Europe's competitiveness and the delivery of services and choice for Europeans.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: We therefore urge the Commission to channel its efforts into:

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: providing clear, practical guidance on existing rules;

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: ensuring coherent interpretation and enforcement of existing laws across Member States to address gaps; and

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: Measuring any additional deficiencies through evidence-based evaluation before considering further expansion of the rulebook.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: We recognise and respect the Commission's commitment to consumer welfare and stand ready to contribute to its next phase of work — whether through

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: technical expertise, collaborative dialogue, or participation in targeted workshops to find policy solutions to clearly evidenced harms.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: A competitiveness-driven approach to consumer policy does not weaken protection; it strengthens it — by ensuring that Europeans benefit from both safe experiences and dynamic, affordable, and innovative digital markets. By focusing on simplification and enforcement, the Commission can deliver a framework that is not only protects consumers, but drives competitiveness and consumer choice as well.

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: Sincerely,

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: Kayvan Hazemi-Jebelli

section_path: 3. Free trials are pro-consumer, and pro-competition
Concluding Remarks
paragraphs.text: Kayvan Hazemi-Jebelli Senior Director for Europe
